name: Dummy Deck
description: Sandbox for testing new technical phrases before migration.

cards.sideA: Me encargué de cambiar el link y poner una imagen en el código como fallback.

sideB.text: I took care of changing the link and putting an image in the code as a fallback.
I handled changing the link and adding an image in the code as a fallback.
I made sure to change the link and place an image in the code as a fallback.

cards.note:
**[pv:173]**

~I took care to change the link and putting an image in the code as a fallback~ is incorrect because it mixes an infinitive ('to change') with a gerund ('putting'). The standard structure uses [take care of] followed by gerunds: [changing] and [putting].

cards.sideA: Podríamos delimitar los alcances y limitaciones.

sideB.text: We could define the scope and limitations.
We could outline the scope and boundaries.
We could narrow down the scope and limitations.
We could delimit the scope and limitations.

cards.note:
**[er:29]**

~We could define the scopes and limitations~ is incorrect. In English, [scope] is generally uncountable when referring to the extent of a project or subject. It remains singular even when paired with plural words like [limitations].

cards.sideA: El maestro cervecero trabaja con pasión.

sideB.text: The master brewer works with passion.
The brewmaster works passionately.
The brewer works with great passion.

cards.sideA: ¿Cómo va el desarrollo de la página estática?

sideB.text: How is the static page coming along?
How is the development of the static page going?
How is the progress on the static page?
How is the static page going?
How is the static page development going?

cards.note:
**[pv:201]**

~How is going the development of the static page?~ has incorrect word order. In questions, the subject must come before the continuous verb: [How is the development... going?].

~How is the development static page going?~ has incorrect word order. When using a compound noun, the broader category goes last: [static page development].

cards.sideA: En la lupa se muestra el fabricante del producto.

sideB.text: The product manufacturer is shown in the search bar.
The product manufacturer is shown using the magnifying glass.
The magnifying glass displays the product's manufacturer.
The manufacturer of the product is displayed in the magnifying glass.
The product maker is shown in the search bar.

cards.sideA: Está obsoleta esa dependencia, por eso la cambiamos.

sideB.text: That dependency is deprecated, which is why we changed it.
That dependency is obsolete, so we replaced it.
That dependency is outdated, that's why we changed it.

cards.sideA: Solo nos faltan 3 fixes para terminar.

sideB.text: We only have 3 fixes left to finish.
There are only 3 fixes left before we are done.
We are just 3 fixes away from being done.
We are just 3 fixes away from finishing.

cards.note:
**[er:108]**

~We are just 3 fixes away to finish~ is incorrect. The phrase 'away from' must be followed by a noun or a gerund (-ing form), not an infinitive. It should be [away from finishing].

cards.sideA: Necesito la guía de Sean para este fix.

sideB.text: I need Sean's guidance for this fix.
I need Sean's input on this fix.
I need Sean to walk me through this fix.

cards.note:
**[er:175]**

~I need the guidance of Sean for this fix~ is grammatically correct but unnatural. For human possession, you must use the Saxon Genitive ([Sean's guidance]). Use 'of' primarily for objects or abstract concepts.

cards.sideA: Necesito la guía de Sean para resolver este problema.

sideB.text: I need Sean's guidance to solve this problem.
I need Sean's help to work through this problem.
I need Sean to guide me in solving this problem.

cards.note:
**[er:175]**

~I need the guidance of Sean to solve this problem~ is unnatural. In English, human possession is expressed with an apostrophe 's. You should say [Sean's guidance].

~I need the Sean's guidance to solve this problem~ is incorrect. Do not use the article 'the' before a proper name with a possessive 's. It should just be [Sean's].

cards.sideA: Bueno, hasta ahora siempre espero el visto bueno.

sideB.text: Well, up until now I always wait for the green light.
Well, up to now I have always waited for approval.
Well, so far I have always waited for the go-ahead.
Well, so far I always wait for the green light.
Well, until now I always wait for the green light.

cards.sideA: Asegurate de que los datos se guarden correctamente.

sideB.text: Ensure that the data is saved properly.
Make sure the data is saved correctly.
Make sure that the data gets saved correctly.

cards.sideA: Ya no estamos mostrando el signo de exclamacion, solo el fallback img.

sideB.text: We are no longer showing the exclamation mark, just the fallback image.
We are no longer displaying the exclamation sign, only the fallback image.
The exclamation mark is no longer showing, only the fallback image.
We are not showing the exclamation mark, just the fallback image.

cards.sideA: No estamos apuntando a Produccion, sino a Soporte.

sideB.text: We are not targeting Production, but rather Support.
We are not aiming for Production, but rather for Support.
We are not aiming for Production, but for Support.

cards.note:
**[er:143]**

~We are not pointing for Production...~ is incorrect. You can point [at] or [to] something physically or metaphorically, but for environments or goals, use [aiming for] or [targeting].

~...aiming for Production, rather, for Support~ is awkwardly punctuated. Use [but rather for] without commas to contrast the targets smoothly.

cards.sideA: Ahi es lo mismo que antes.

sideB.text: That is the same as before.
It is the same as it was before.
That is just the same as before.

cards.note:
~There is the same as before~ is incorrect. [There] refers to a physical location. To say "ahí" in the abstract sense of "in that aspect" or "in that case", you must use [That] (e.g., [That is the same...]).

[There] can refer to digital locations (e.g., 'Put the cursor there'), but it cannot act as the subject of the sentence to mean 'that place/aspect'. Saying 'There is the same' translates to the existential 'Hay el mismo'. To say 'esa parte' or 'ese aspecto' is the same, you must use a demonstrative pronoun like [That] as the subject, or use 'there' as an adverb at the end: [It is the same there].

cards.sideA: Solo debes comentar esa linea, y descomentar la otra.

sideB.text: You just need to comment out that line and uncomment the other one.
Just comment out that line and uncomment the other one.
All you need to do is comment out that line and uncomment the other.

cards.note:
**[er:221]**
**[pv:202]**

~You just need to comment that line and uncomment the other one~ is incorrect in a programming context. The verb [comment] means to express an opinion. To disable a line of code, you must use the phrasal verb [comment out].

cards.sideA: Este procedimiento fue completamente reescrito.

sideB.text: This procedure was completely rewritten.
This procedure has been completely rewritten.
The procedure was entirely rewritten.
This procedure was completely redone.

cards.sideA: Hasta ahora han habido tantos cambios en el paquete.

sideB.text: There have been so many changes to the package so far.
So many changes have been made to the package up to this point.
The package has gone through so many changes up to now.
Up to now, there have been so many changes in the package.
So far, there have been so many changes in the package.

cards.sideA: Han habido tantos cambios, que no habiamos comentado lo realizado.

sideB.text: There have been so many changes that we hadn't commented what was done.
There were so many changes that we forgot to add comments for what was completed.
So many changes happened that we didn't document what was done in the comments.
There have been so many changes that we haven't left comments explaining what was done.

cards.sideA: El dropdown usa un multiplicador basado en el conversion rate.

sideB.text: The dropdown uses a multiplier based on the conversion rate.
The dropdown applies a multiplier that is based on the conversion rate.
The dropdown relies on a multiplier based on the conversion rate.
The dropdown works with a multiplier derived from the conversion rate.

cards.sideA: Han pasado 5 minutos y nada.

sideB.text: Five minutes have passed and nothing.
It has been 5 minutes and there is nothing.
Five minutes went by and still nothing.
Five minutes have gone by and still no response.

cards.note: ~There have passed five minutes and nothing~ is unnatural. Do not use 'there have passed' for time. The correct structure places the time as the subject: [Five minutes have passed].

cards.sideA: Han pasado un par de minutos, y no responde.

sideB.text: A couple of minutes have passed and it is not responding.
A few minutes have gone by and there is still no response.
It has been a couple of minutes and it is still not responding.
A couple of minutes passed and there is still no answer.

cards.note: ~There have passed some minutes and it is not responding~ is unnatural because English does not use 'there' with 'to pass' for elapsed time. You should start with the time itself: [Some minutes have passed] or [It has been some minutes].

cards.sideA: Me quedan 2 minutos para terminarlo.

sideB.text: I have 2 minutes left to finish it.
I only have 2 minutes left to complete it.
There are 2 minutes left for me to finish it.
I am left with 2 minutes to wrap it up.

cards.sideA: Esta tardando mas de lo normal.

sideB.text: It is taking longer than usual.
It is taking more time than normal.
This is taking longer than it normally does.
It is taking more time than expected.

cards.sideA: Si es un problema aislado, deberiamos consultar con el equipo.

sideB.text: If it is an isolated issue, we should check with the team.
If this is an isolated problem, we should consult with the team.
Should this be an isolated issue, we should reach out to the team.
If it is a one-off problem, we should get in touch with the team.

cards.sideA: Pero no es un problema aislado, ya ha pasado varias veces.

sideB.text: But it is not an isolated issue, it has already happened several times.
But this is not an isolated problem, it has occurred multiple times.
This is not an isolated issue, it has happened several times already.
But it is not a one-off problem, it has come up several times.

cards.note: ~But it is not an isolated issue, it has already passed several times~ is incorrect here. In English, events [happen] or [occur]. Time 'passes', but situations or errors do not.

cards.sideA: Necesitas usar guion, no guion bajo.

sideB.text: You need to use a hyphen, not an underscore.
You should use a dash, not an underscore.
Make sure you use a hyphen, not an underscore.
Use a hyphen, not an underscore.

cards.sideA: Necesitamos aislar el problema para proceder.

sideB.text: We need to isolate the issue in order to proceed.
We have to isolate the problem before we can move forward.
We need to narrow down the problem to proceed.
We must isolate the issue to be able to continue.

cards.sideA: Puedes aislar y encontrar la causa del problema?

sideB.text: Can you isolate and find the root cause of the problem?
Are you able to isolate and identify the cause of the issue?
Can you narrow it down and find out what is causing the problem?
Could you isolate the issue and determine its cause?

cards.sideA: Me encargare de eso, y te hare saber acerca de ello.

sideB.text: I will take care of that and let you know about it.
I will handle it and keep you posted.
I will deal with it and update you on the matter.
I will take care of it and get back to you about it.

cards.sideA: Me encargue de cambiarlo por otro mejor.

sideB.text: I took care of replacing it with a better one.
I handled swapping it out for a better option.
I was in charge of changing it for a better one.
I took it upon myself to replace it with something better.
I took care of changing it for a better one.

cards.sideA: ¿Necesito poner guion, o guión bajo?

sideB.text: Do I need to use a hyphen or an underscore?
Should I put a hyphen or an underscore?
Do I need to put a dash or an underscore?

cards.sideA: ¿Necesito usar comillas simples o dobles?

sideB.text: Do I need to use single or double quotes?
Should I use single or double quotation marks?
Is it necessary to use single or double quotes?

cards.sideA: No se cómo se llama este signo, dejame pegarlo en el chat.

sideB.text: I don't know what this symbol is called, let me paste it in the chat.
I don't know the name of this sign, let me paste it into the chat.
I'm not sure what this character is called, let me paste it in the chat.

cards.sideA: ¿Cómo puedo llamar a este signo?, dejame pegarlo en el chat.

sideB.text: What can I call this symbol? Let me paste it in the chat.
How should I call this sign? Let me paste it into the chat.
How can I describe this symbol? Let me paste it in the chat.

cards.sideA: Los datos se sobreescribían automáticamente

sideB.text: The data was overwritten automatically.
The data used to be overwritten automatically.
The data got overwritten automatically.

cards.sideA: Ahora limpiamos los datos, y se popular desde cero.

sideB.text: Now we clear the data and populate it from scratch.
Now we wipe the data and it gets populated from scratch.
Now we clean the data and it populates from the ground up.

cards.sideA: Antes gracias a qué se sobreescribían los datos no había error.

sideB.text: Before, since the data was overwritten, there was no error.
Previously, thanks to the data being overwritten, there was no error.
Before, because the data would overwrite automatically, there wasn't an error.

cards.sideA: Antes no se populaban desde cero, se sobreescribían.

sideB.text: Before, they weren't populated from scratch, they were overwritten.
Previously, it didn't populate from scratch; it was overwritten.
Before, the data wouldn't populate from scratch; it just got overwritten.
